decks.title: Jobs and Workplace Vocabulary
decks.summary: This homework covers common workplace and profession vocabulary in Mandarin. Students will learn how to talk about companies, offices, and various jobs. The lesson also introduces the measure word 'jia' for companies and stores, and a useful sentence for asking about someone's occupation.

cards.example: wǒ zài yì jiā gōngsī gōngzuò

cards.meaning: measure word for companies, stores, restaurants
cards.notes: Used when counting businesses or establishments
General term for doctor
Very common in Taiwan
Refers to Western/modern medicine
Used to call a server in a restaurant
Can be used as noun or verb
Used here to mean 'do' in the context of work

decks.title: Asking for Directions
decks.summary: This homework covers essential vocabulary and phrases for asking and giving directions in Mandarin. Students will learn how to say left, right, straight, and how to politely ask for directions to a place. Useful everyday phrases like getting on/off transportation and saying goodbye are also included.

cards.notes: Used before a direction to indicate 'toward that direction'
More complete/polite way to say turn left
Often used with 'de' to mean 'near something'

cards.meaning: get on (a bus, train, plane, etc.)
get off (a bus, train, plane, etc.)
excuse me (polite way to ask a question)
cards.notes: Very useful phrase to start any question to a stranger
Common expression of happiness or relief
Reduplicated form for emphasis
Literally 'walk slowly'; a friendly way to say goodbye

decks.title: Transportation and Daily Commuting
decks.summary: This homework covers vocabulary and grammar related to transportation and daily commuting. The student will learn how to talk about different modes of transport (bus, train, subway, taxi, bike, motorcycle, boat, plane), describe commuting experiences, and express opinions about convenience and comfort. The material also practices time expressions and comparing experiences in different cities.

cards.notes: used for buses, trains, taxis, planes, boats

cards.example: wǒ měitiān kāichē yí ge xiǎoshí
wǒ lèi de shíhou huì zuò huǒchē
měitiān dōu zuò dìtiě qù shàngbān

cards.notes: very common in Taiwan

cards.example: zuò chuán cóng Táiwān dào Xiānggǎng
wǒ bú tài xǐhuan zuò fēijī

cards.notes: used for things you straddle

cards.example: yě huì zǒulù huòshì qí zìxíngchē
zuò chuán yòu ānjìng yòu shūfu
zuò chuán yòu ānjìng yòu shūfu
wǒ lèi de shíhou huì zuò huǒchē
ǒu'ěr wǒ lèi de shíhou huì zuò huǒchē
měitiān zuò gōnggòng qìchē qù gōngzuò
rúguǒ yǒu wèizi wǒ huì zuòxià
wǒ zhù zài Jiùjīnshān de jiāowài
Jiùjīnshān de chūzūchē bǐ Niǔyuē guì

decks.title: Shopping & Phone Calls - Chit Chat Chinese Ch. 11 Continued
decks.summary: This homework covers shopping vocabulary and dialogues (buying clothes, asking prices, trying things on), making phone calls and plans in Mandarin, the V-yi-V pattern for 'giving something a try,' and using 'zai' to express ongoing actions (-ing). Cultural notes on colors in Chinese culture are also included.

cards.example: wǒ xiǎng mǎi yī jiàn chènshān
cards.notes: 3rd tone; don't confuse with mài (to sell)

cards.example: zhèi jiā shāngdiàn mài bù mài wàitào?
cards.notes: 4th tone; same sound as mǎi but different tone

cards.meaning: measure word for stores, restaurants & businesses
cards.notes: same character as family/home
said by staff when you enter a business

cards.meaning: it's OK, it's alright, it's fine
cards.notes: implies something is acceptable but not ideal

cards.example: zhèi tiáo hēisè de kùzi hěn liúxíng

cards.notes: used in business, to elders, doctors, bosses

cards.meaning: give it a try, try it on
cards.example: nín kěyǐ shì yī shì zhèi jiàn
cards.notes: V-yi-V pattern
literally: hit + telephone + give
used only for phone greetings
used when calling for someone on the phone

cards.example: qǐng děng yīxià, nǐ de diànhuà

cards.notes: literally: see + face

cards.meaning: to pick someone up (at a place)

cards.notes: bù xíng = not possible
same word for both borrowing and lending
literally: no need for politeness

cards.example: wǒ xiǎng mǎi yī jiàn wàitào

decks.title: Colors, Clothes, Money & Modifiers
decks.summary: This homework covers color vocabulary, clothing items with their measure words, how to say prices in Mandarin, the use of 'de' as a descriptive particle between adjectives and nouns, and the modifier pattern 'adj. + yidian' meaning 'a bit more.'

cards.meaning: to put on (a piece of clothing)

cards.notes: measure word: tiáo
measure word: shuāng
measure word: dǐng
measure word: shuāng
measure word: jiàn
measure word: shuāng
measure word: tiáo

cards.meaning: how much money / how much is it
cards.notes: literally: many-few money

decks.title: Restaurant Dining and Useful Expressions
decks.summary: This lesson covers useful everyday expressions (like 'anything is fine,' 'let's go,' 'please sit down'), restaurant vocabulary (food, drinks, ordering), measure words for food and beverages, and practice dialogues about going out to eat at a Chinese restaurant.

cards.example: dōu kěyǐ = anything is fine, either one is fine

cards.meaning: it doesn't matter (to me), I don't care
cards.example: wǒ yě wúsuǒwèi = I don't care either

cards.meaning: it's up to you, whatever you like
cards.notes: literally 'follow convenience'

cards.example: nàme, wǒmen qù chīfàn ba = in that case, let's go eat
qǐng lái càidān = please bring a menu
bùyòng kèqi = no need to be so polite
nǐmen jǐ wèi? = how many of you are there?

cards.notes: literally 'open door'

cards.example: tāmen jǐ diǎn guānmén? = what time do they close?
cards.notes: literally 'close door'

cards.example: qǐng lái càidān = please bring a menu
xiǎojiě, mǎidān = miss, the bill please
liǎng bēi chá = two cups of tea
qǐng lái yì píng píjiǔ = please bring a bottle of beer

cards.notes: Chinese say 'hē tāng' (drink soup), not 'eat soup'

cards.example: wǒmen lái gānbēi ba = let's cheers
cards.notes: sometimes literally means empty your glass

decks.title: Introductions and Nationalities
decks.summary: This homework covers how to introduce yourself, ask someone's name and surname, and ask/state nationalities. It includes formal and informal greetings, yes/no questions with 'ma', question words like 'shenme', 'na', and 'shei', and the abbreviated question particle 'ne'.

cards.notes: Used when addressing elders or in formal situations

cards.meaning: May I ask your last name?
cards.notes: Polite way to ask someone's surname
Used as both a verb and noun
Can also mean 'husband' in everyday speech
Used for full name or given name
Used in greetings

cards.example: ni shi Li xian sheng ma?
cards.notes: Added at end of statement to make it a yes/no question
Changes to 2nd tone before another 4th tone: bu shi becomes bu(2) shi

cards.example: ta bu shi Ying guo ren
ni shi Li Ai mei ma?
ni jiao shen me ming zi?
cards.notes: Can refer to full name or first name
Sometimes pronounced 'nei'
Used for follow-up questions
Sounds the same as 'he' in spoken Mandarin
Refers to people of Chinese descent living abroad
Common greeting for teachers
